narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 63% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on a careful look at your academic record, test scores, background, and other details compared to thousands of similar students. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3rd—lower than the typical student's 5.53rd—stood out as the single most influential factor, gently nudging the model toward expecting a failure rather than a pass. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 5th, also below the average of about 5.71st, reinforcing that early concern about your relative class performance. Your LSAT score of 33, well under the usual 36.7, came next in importance, similarly tipping the scales toward a riskier outlook. Factors like your middle family income bracket, White racial background, undergraduate GPA of 3.20 (very close to the average 3.21), part-time work plans, and male gender played smaller roles but all contributed in the same direction, pushing the prediction slightly more toward failure than success. Overall, your profile shows solid but consistently below-average academic markers in key areas when stacked against those who typically pass the bar, with no standout strengths to offset them. While your GPA is right on par, the lower deciles and LSAT created the biggest drags compared to peers. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your grades to climb higher in your class deciles, as that's the top factor and something you can directly influence through better study habits or courses. Retaking the LSAT for a score closer to or above 37 could make a huge difference, since it's a close second in impact and highly changeable. Switching to full-time enrollment might help too, as part-time paths are rarer among successful bar takers. With targeted improvements here, you'd stand out more favorably against the averages and likely flip the model's view. Keep pushing—you've got a strong foundation to build on.
